what_it_does: Implements a React component, MorphStage, that animates transitions between stages by keeping the outgoing children as a frozen 'ghost' layer while a new incoming layer enters, so the UI appears to reform rather than swap pages.
observations: Aliases React hooks with a 'Morph' suffix (useStateMorph, useEffectMorph, useRefMorph).
Supports three layout variants: 'screen' (fixed full-viewport), 'fill' (absolutely-stacked), and 'flow' (default, incoming drives layout).
Tracks two layers in state: 'cur' (incoming, phases 'enter' -> 'in') and 'leaving' (outgoing ghost, phases 'hold' -> 'out').
Uses a childrenRef to capture the previous render's children for the ghost snapshot.
Promotes phases with a double requestAnimationFrame plus a setTimeout fallback, and immediately settles if the document is hidden.
Respects prefers-reduced-motion by skipping the 'enter'/'hold' phases and using a 0ms retire timeout.
Renders the outgoing layer with aria-hidden='true' and the incoming layer live.
standouts: The 'ghost' pattern: outgoing children are frozen as a node and recede while the incoming surface reforms.
Belt-and-braces scheduling for phase promotion (rAF + setTimeout + document.hidden guard).
Reduced-motion short-circuit built into the state machine.
conventions: Effect A reacts to key changes; Effect B updates the previous-children ref afterward.
CSS class names follow a BEM-ish pattern: morph-stage, morph-layer, morph-layer--enter/in/hold/out, morph--{motion}.
concerns: Relies on declaration order of useEffect hooks for childrenRef correctness, which is fragile if refactored.
If children change without a stageKey change, the ghost may snapshot stale content.
No validation of the 'motion' or 'variant' props.
The reduced-motion path sets state phases directly but still depends on CSS honoring the preference.
notes: A transition primitive that freezes the old view and cross-fades/morphs in the new one, with scheduling guards for hidden tabs and reduced motion.
questions: Where are the CSS rules that define the morph-layer transitions?
How should focus, scroll position, and accessibility be handled during the ghost phase?
What is the intended behavior when children change while stageKey remains the same?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the implementation: it holds an outgoing ghost and reforms an incoming surface rather than teleporting between screens.
apparent_themes: Resilience to throttling and hidden tabs
intention_signals: It is trying to make navigation feel like a single elastic surface reshaping itself, preserving visual context across state changes.
novelty: The explicit ghost-layer state machine that freezes the previous children as a renderable node and coordinates enter/hold/out phases through React state rather than just CSS animations.
contribution: A reusable, self-contained transition component that a fused system could keep to provide staged morphing between screens, workspaces, or wizard steps with reduced-motion and hidden-tab safeguards.
summary_for_embedding: MorphStage is a React transition component that freezes outgoing children as an aria-hidden ghost layer and animates an incoming layer through enter/in phases using a state machine with rAF/setTimeout scheduling and prefers-reduced-motion handling.
intention_for_embedding: This file was written to replace abrupt page swaps with a continuous, surface-reforming transition experience across screens, workspaces, and wizard steps.

symbols.does: Promotes the incoming layer from the 'enter' phase to the 'in' phase if it is still entering.
symbols.description: A local function scheduled inside a useEffect that finalizes the incoming layer's entrance animation by updating cur.phase from 'enter' to 'in', guarded so it only runs when the layer is still in the enter phase.

symbols.does: Promotes the outgoing ghost layer from the 'hold' phase to the 'out' phase if it is still being held.
symbols.description: A local function scheduled inside a useEffect that starts the outgoing layer's exit animation by updating leaving.phase from 'hold' to 'out', guarded so it only runs when the ghost is still in the hold phase.
